title: Entire Phase-8 type block (Relation, FrameRole, CameraPose, FrameSetMember, FrameSet, BurstGroup, ComposeBurstFrame, defaultComposeBurstFrame, getSharpnessRank, argmaxSharpness, AssetChannel, ChannelDescriptor) is duplicated verbatim at lines 263–411, causing two identical export declarations in the same file
description: Lines 119–207 define and export Relation, FrameRole, CameraPose, FrameSetMember, FrameSet, BurstGroup, ComposeBurstFrame, defaultComposeBurstFrame. Lines 263–357 copy the exact same interfaces and functions a second time under the same export names. TypeScript will surface a 'Duplicate identifier' error for each name, which means the compiled output is either broken or only one declaration survives. Any consumer of the exported types is relying on the first declaration; the second shadows or collides with it depending on the TS version and module target. This is a latent build-breakage bug in the public API surface.

title: selectTiers may return tiers with byteEnd === 0 when dcEvent.byteOffset is 0, violating the manifest contract that byteEnd > 0 for a usable tier
description: In selectTiers, the dc tier is assigned byteEnd = dcEvent.byteOffset (line 72). If the session fires a frame at byteOffset 0 (possible when bytesPushed is read before the first push completes due to the Promise.resolve() microtask yield), dcEvent.byteOffset is 0. The resulting ManifestTier has byteEnd = 0. validateManifest does not range-check byteEnd > 0 and will accept the zero value. Downstream, fetchTier(url, tier, ...) calls fromRangePrefix(url, 0, ...) which issues a Range: bytes=0--1 header — an invalid RFC 7233 range that most servers reject with 416. The tier appears valid but is unfetchable.

title: validateManifest does not check byteStart <= byteEnd or byteEnd > 0 on any tier, so invalid range values pass validation and produce unfetchable or undefined-behaviour tiers
description: The manifest schema (ManifestTier) carries byteStart and byteEnd as raw numbers. validateManifest verifies their types but not their values: byteEnd could be 0, negative, or less than byteStart. A manifest written with byteEnd=0 on the dc tier passes validateManifest and is cached and dispatched via onManifest. fetchTier will issue a Range: bytes=0--1 header, and fetchTierWithPrefix will hit the prefixLength >= tier.byteEnd early-close path incorrectly (prefix 0 >= byteEnd 0 is true, so the session is closed before any fetch). Adding assertField(t.byteEnd > t.byteStart, ...) would close this.

title: When no manifestTier exists (fallback fetchFull path), job.currentTier is set to job.targetTier rather than the literal tier that was fetched, allowing a jump of multiple tiers in one step
description: At line 664: const achieved = manifestTier !== undefined ? target : job.targetTier. In the no-manifest case, target = nextTier(job.currentTier) which is one step up, but achieved = job.targetTier which may be 'full' while currentTier is 'none' — a two-step jump. This means onTier fires with 'full' while 'dc' and 'preview' were never acknowledged. Consumers that gate behaviour on tier sequence (e.g. quality-progressive rendering) will see contract violations. The correct value in the no-manifest path is target (the tier actually attempted and fetched), not job.targetTier.

title: ProgressiveGallery.observe() silently drops the call when jobs.size >= maxQueuedJobs with no notification to the caller, leaking the expectation that the image will be displayed
description: When the gallery is at capacity (line 212: if (this.jobs.size >= this.opts.maxQueuedJobs) return), observe() returns void without any indication of failure. The caller's element is not registered with IntersectionObserver and no frames or tier callbacks will ever fire for it. There is no onError call, no thrown exception, and no returned status. Any UI component that calls observe() and expects at least one onFrame/onTier callback will wait indefinitely, breaking the public contract that observe(element, id, url) eventually leads to a decode cycle for visible elements.

title: session.push(startingPrefix) is called with (session as any).push(), bypassing the DecodeSession type and ignoring the returned Promise, so push errors are silently swallowed and the session is not awaited
description: Line 572: (session as any).push(startingPrefix). The cast to any circumvents TypeScript's type safety and the Promise returned by push() is not awaited. If push() throws or rejects (e.g. session already closed, WASM OOM), the error is silently lost and the decode continues as if the prefix was fed successfully — leading to a corrupted progressive decode state. The CLAUDE.md contract states push() is synchronous from WASM's perspective, but the DecodeSession interface declares push() async; the fire-and-forget here is still a contract violation in the session's own async envelope.

title: getManifest writes an empty ArrayBuffer as a 'sentinel for eviction' but the underlying JxlCacheBrowser.set() contract does not guarantee that a zero-length value is evicted; a subsequent get() may return an empty ArrayBuffer that trips the byteLength === 0 guard and silently returns null instead of propagating a real error
description: Line 62: void this.inner.set(key, new ArrayBuffer(0)). The comment says 'jxl-cache LRU will drop it' but the JxlCacheBrowser interface has no such contract. If the LRU does not evict zero-length entries (e.g. because eviction is size-based and 0 counts as the cheapest entry to keep), a subsequent getManifest() will find the empty buffer, fall into the parse-failure catch, and return null — which is functionally correct but masks the stale-entry condition. Worse, invalidate() uses the same empty-buffer sentinel, meaning a legitimate 'not found' and a 'deliberately invalidated' state are indistinguishable. setManifest right after invalidate() races with the eviction and may store over the sentinel, appearing to restore a manifest.

title: toSaliency returns a Saliency with confidence omitted when opts.confidence is undefined, but ProgressiveManifest.saliency declares confidence as a required number — writing the result directly into a manifest produces a schema violation
description: The Saliency interface (line 83) declares confidence as optional (confidence?: number), but ProgressiveManifest.saliency (progressive-manifest.ts line 43-49) declares confidence: number (required, no ?). toSaliency conditionally omits confidence when opts.confidence is undefined (line 126: ...(opts.confidence !== undefined ? { confidence: opts.confidence } : {})). A caller following the documented usage 'profileJxl(..., { saliency: toSaliency(bestCenter) })' will pass a saliency object missing confidence into profileJxl, which stores it verbatim. The resulting manifest then fails validateManifest's confidence type check at retrieval time, causing getManifest to return null as if the manifest never existed.

title: prefetchManifest races with startDecode: both paths call fetchAndCacheManifest independently; if prefetch completes after startDecode checks job.manifest === null, both write to the cache and startDecode uses whichever manifest it fetched, potentially discarding a prefetch result
description: prefetchManifest (line 427) sets job.manifestChecked = true and fires fetchAndCacheManifest. Concurrently, startDecode (lines 500-506) checks job.manifest === null && !job.manifestChecked before calling fetchAndCacheManifest. The sequence is: prefetchManifest sets manifestChecked=true; startDecode runs; startDecode sees manifestChecked=true and skips its own fetch; prefetch .then sets job.manifest — correct. However if prefetchManifest fires but job.manifest is still null when startDecode runs its inner check at line 503 (job.manifest === null && !job.manifestChecked), and manifestChecked is true, startDecode skips the fetch and proceeds with manifest = null. If the prefetch later returns a manifest it is written to job.manifest (line 433) but manifestDispatched may already be true from a previous decode cycle, so onManifest never fires for the freshly-fetched manifest.

category: enum not handled by all consumers
title: nextTier returns null for 'full' but the Tier type includes 'full'; callers that skip the null-check can call startDecode on a job already at 'full', resulting in a no-op startDecode that still increments activeDecoders
description: nextTier(current: Tier): Tier | null returns null only for 'full'. In startDecode, line 521: const target = nextTier(job.currentTier); if (target === null) return. The return is inside startDecode after activeDecoders has already been incremented (line 487). The finally block decrements it, so the count stays correct. However tick() at line 472-473 filters candidates with tierRank(j.currentTier) < tierRank(j.targetTier), which should prevent full-tier jobs from entering the queue. If targetTier is 'full' and currentTier is 'full' the filter correctly excludes the job. The gap is: setTargetTier(id, 'none') is not prevented at the public API level — a caller can set targetTier below currentTier, which causes the tick filter to exclude the job permanently without any notification.

title: profileJxlFile writes the manifest even when opts.writeManifest is explicitly false only because the condition is opts.writeManifest !== false; if writeManifest is undefined (default), the manifest is written — but the public ProfileOptions type does not document writeManifest, creating an undocumented implicit write-by-default behavior
description: profileJxlFile is exported as a public API. Its opts parameter type is ProfileOptions & { writeManifest?: boolean }. The extra writeManifest field is not part of the exported ProfileOptions type declared in the public API. The write behavior is default-on (line 208: if (opts.writeManifest !== false)), meaning callers who pass no writeManifest option will have a .json file silently written beside their .jxl file. This side effect is undocumented in the exported type and surprising to callers who just want the in-memory manifest. The type extension is only visible by reading the implementation.

title: evictBitmaps prefix-strips jxlUrl from bitmap keys using lastIndexOf('#') but the keep-set is built without the '#tier' suffix, so a jxlUrl that itself contains '#' (valid in HTTP URLs for fragment) will produce an incorrect keep-set and may retain or evict the wrong entries
description: evictBitmaps builds keep as a Set of BITMAP_KEY_PREFIX + jxlUrl (no tier suffix). For each key it strips from the last '#' to get urlPart. If jxlUrl contains a '#' fragment (e.g. 'https://example.com/img.jxl#section'), urlPart will be truncated at that '#' and not match the keep set. The eviction will incorrectly delete bitmaps for URLs that should be kept, or retain bitmaps for URLs that should be evicted. This is a contract gap: the cache accepts arbitrary jxlUrl strings from callers but the internal key scheme is fragile against '#' in the URL.

title: makeJob fixture includes prefixChunks: [] which is not a field in the ProgressiveImageJob interface (the interface uses prefixAccum/prefixBytes), so any test that spreads makeJob into startDecode will have a stale field that is silently ignored
description: The ProgressiveImageJob interface (progressive-scheduler.ts lines 24-54) defines prefixAccum: Uint8Array | null and prefixBytes: number. There is no prefixChunks field. The test makeJob (line 123) includes prefixChunks: [] — an obsolete field from a prior design. Several tests (e.g. D-4 at line 384, E-1 at line 535) also set job.prefixChunks = [] directly. Since the field does not exist on the interface, these assignments are dead code. Any test that relies on the chunk-array approach for seeding prefix data is not actually exercising the real code path — the real path reads prefixAccum/prefixBytes. The tests pass only because they also manually override job.currentTier at the end.

title: validateManifest does not validate the capture, channels, or channelDescriptors optional fields introduced for Phase 8, leaving injected CV data completely unvalidated at the manifest boundary
description: ProgressiveManifest now includes three optional Phase-8 fields: capture (CameraPose, intrinsics, extrinsics, depthLayer, featureSidecar), channels (AssetChannel[]), and channelDescriptors (ChannelDescriptor[]). validateManifest performs no checks on any of these fields. A manifest with a malformed capture.pose.lat = 'hello' or channels = [42] passes validation and is cached and dispatched. Consumers expecting typed data will receive unvalidated garbage. Adding at least array-of-string checks for channels and typeof checks for pose numerics would close this gap without breaking forwards-compatibility.

title: migrateManifest throws on version > 1 but has no migration path; there is no version negotiation protocol for clients to signal which versions they can decode, so a version-2 manifest from a newer server hard-crashes all older clients
description: migrateManifest currently throws ManifestValidationError for any version > 1. There is no capability negotiation: a client that fetches a v2 manifest will throw rather than degrade gracefully. The public API exposes no mechanism to query 'what manifest versions can this client handle'. An opportunity to add a maxVersion parameter to validateManifest/migrateManifest, or expose a SUPPORTED_MANIFEST_VERSIONS constant, would allow callers to provide a user-friendly error or fallback rather than a hard throw. This is especially relevant now that Phase 8 fields are landing, which may require a version bump.
